response_text:
# JavaScript's Concurrency Model

## The Honest Answer First

**JavaScript does not have traditional multi-threading.** It has a **single-threaded execution model** with an event-driven concurrency system. This is a common misconception worth addressing directly.

---

## The Single-Threaded Reality

```javascript
// All of this runs on ONE thread, sequentially
function blockingOperation() {
    const start = Date.now();
    while (Date.now() - start < 3000) {} // Blocks EVERYTHING for 3 seconds
    console.log("Done blocking");
}

console.log("Start");
blockingOperation();        // UI freezes, no events processed
console.log("End");         // Only runs after blocking completes

// Output:
// Start
// (3 second freeze)
// Done blocking
// End
```

---

## The Event Loop: How Concurrency Is Simulated

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    JavaScript Runtime                    │
│                                                          │
│  ┌──────────────┐    ┌─────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │  Call Stack  │    │        Web APIs             │    │
│  │              │    │  (setTimeout, fetch, DOM)   │    │
│  │  main()      │    │                             │    │
│  │  foo()       │    │  These run OUTSIDE the      │    │
│  │  bar()       │    │  JS thread (C++ / OS level) │    │
│  └──────┬───────┘    └──────────────┬──────────────┘    │
│         │                           │                    │
│         │            ┌──────────────▼──────────────┐    │
│         │            │       Callback Queue        │    │
│         │            │  [cb1, cb2, cb3...]         │    │
│         │            └──────────────┬──────────────┘    │
│         │                           │                    │
│         └──────────── Event Loop
